lessons.title: Python Basics & Output — Your First Forensic Tool

theory.introduction:
Welcome to Python — the most popular programming language in cybersecurity, digital forensics, and OSINT investigations! If Java is the armoured tank of programming, Python is the Swiss Army knife — lightweight, flexible, and incredibly powerful. Every forensic analyst, penetration tester, and security researcher uses Python daily. Tools like Autopsy plugins, Volatility memory forensics, Scapy network analysis, and hundreds of OSINT tools are ALL built with Python.

Think of it this way: when a detective arrives at a crime scene, the first thing they do is DOCUMENT everything — take photos, write notes, label evidence bags. In Python, the first thing you learn is print() — the command that DOCUMENTS your findings by displaying them on screen. It sounds simple, but EVERY forensic tool you'll ever build starts with print().

In this topic, you'll learn to write Python code that displays text, formats output, and builds the foundation for real forensic tools. By the end, you'll be creating structured evidence reports, formatted log entries, and investigator-style output — and you'll understand why Python is the language of choice for security professionals worldwide.

IMPORTANT: For hands-on practice with real forensic tools later in this course, we recommend setting up a Kali Linux virtual machine (VM) — it's free from kali.org. Synapse teaches you the Python concepts safely here, and you can apply them with real tools like exiftool, steghide, Wireshark, and Nmap in your VM. Never run security tools on networks you don't own!

As you progress through the Synapse Python course, you will learn to extract hidden GPS coordinates from photographs, decode secret messages hidden inside images using steganography, analyse network packets to detect intrusions, crack password hashes, parse server logs for suspicious activity, and automate security scans. But everything — absolutely everything — starts with understanding how Python displays output. Master print() and f-strings here, and the rest of the course will feel natural and exciting.

TOOL TIP: On your Kali Linux VM, try running these commands to see real forensic tools in action:
  exiftool photo.jpg     (extracts image metadata including GPS coordinates)
  steghide info image.jpg (checks if an image contains hidden data)
  strings suspicious.exe  (extracts readable text from any file — great for malware analysis)
  xxd file.bin | head     (shows hexadecimal dump of any file)

You will learn to BUILD Python versions of tools like these throughout this course!
theory.why_important: Python is the #1 language for cybersecurity professionals. According to the SANS Institute, over 80% of security tools are written in or scriptable with Python. Digital forensic investigators use Python to extract metadata from images, parse log files, decode hidden messages, and automate evidence collection. OSINT researchers use it to scrape websites, trace IP addresses, and map social networks. Even the most basic print() function becomes powerful when you use it to display extracted GPS coordinates, decoded hex values, or parsed network packets. Learning Python output is step one toward becoming a forensic analyst, penetration tester, or security engineer.

key_concepts.concept: The print() Function — Your Evidence Display
key_concepts.explanation:
print() is Python's built-in function for displaying output to the screen. Think of it like a forensic photographer's camera — it captures what's inside and makes it visible for everyone to see. Without print(), your Python code runs silently — like an investigation with no report. Everything inside the parentheses gets displayed on screen.

In forensics, print() is used to display extracted metadata, decoded messages, analysis results, and investigation findings. It's the foundation of every forensic script you'll ever write.
key_concepts.example:
# Your very first Python line!
print("Welcome to Synapse — Python Forensics")
print("Evidence extraction starting...")
print("Analysis complete!")
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of print() as your forensic notebook — it records and displays your findings. Every investigation tool starts here!

key_concepts.explanation:
A string is text enclosed in quotes. In Python, you can use single quotes ('hello'), double quotes ("hello"), or triple quotes ('''hello''' or """hello"""). They all create strings! Think of strings like evidence labels — the quotes are the evidence bag, and the text inside is the evidence itself.

In digital forensics, strings are EVERYWHERE: filenames, IP addresses, email addresses, URLs, timestamps, hash values, error messages, and user data are ALL strings. Mastering strings means mastering evidence handling.
key_concepts.example:
# Different quote styles — all valid!
print('Single quotes work fine')
print("Double quotes work too")
print('''Triple quotes allow
multiple lines
of text''')
key_concepts.ai_hint: Single or double quotes? Use whichever you prefer! But if your text contains one type of quote, use the other type to wrap it.

key_concepts.explanation:
Comments are notes in your code that Python ignores completely. They start with # for single lines. Think of them like sticky notes on a case file — they help you (and other investigators) understand what each piece of code does and WHY you wrote it that way.

In professional forensic scripts, comments are CRITICAL. They document your methodology, explain your reasoning, and create an audit trail. A forensic script without comments is like an investigation report without explanation — no court would accept it.
key_concepts.example:
# CASE: Metadata extraction from suspect's phone images
# INVESTIGATOR: Your Name
# DATE: 2025-02-28

# Step 1: Display case header
print("=" * 50)
print("DIGITAL FORENSICS REPORT")
print("=" * 50)
key_concepts.ai_hint: Professional forensic analysts ALWAYS comment their code. It's not optional — it's part of the evidence chain. Make it a habit now!

key_concepts.concept: Escape Characters — Special Codes in Text
key_concepts.explanation:
Escape characters are special codes that start with a backslash (\). They tell Python to do something special inside a string: \n creates a new line, \t creates a tab, \\ prints an actual backslash, and \" prints a quote mark. Think of them like secret codes in a message — they look like normal text but trigger special behaviour.

In forensics, escape characters are essential for formatting log output, creating structured reports, handling file paths (especially on Windows with backslashes), and parsing data that contains special characters.
key_concepts.example:
# Escape characters in action
print("Evidence:
	File: photo.jpg
	Size: 2.4MB
	Path: C:\Users\suspect\Desktop")
print("Investigator said: "Check the metadata"")
key_concepts.ai_hint: \n = new line, \t = tab indent. These are your formatting superpowers! Use them to make output readable and professional.

key_concepts.explanation:
f-strings (formatted string literals) let you embed variables and expressions INSIDE strings using curly braces {}. Just put an 'f' before the opening quote: f"text {variable} more text". Think of them like a form template — the curly braces are blank fields that get filled in automatically.

In forensic scripts, f-strings are used EVERYWHERE: "File: {filename}, Hash: {md5_hash}, Size: {filesize} bytes". They make your output dynamic — the same code can report on different evidence. This is how real forensic tools generate their reports.
key_concepts.example:
# f-strings — the modern way to format output
case_number = "CASE-2025-0042"
evidence_count = 15
status = "ACTIVE"

print(f"Case: {case_number}")
print(f"Evidence items: {evidence_count}")
print(f"Status: {status}")
print(f"Items remaining: {evidence_count - 3}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: f-strings are the most popular way to format text in modern Python. The 'f' stands for 'formatted'. Master this and your output will look professional!

key_concepts.explanation:
You can join (concatenate) strings using the + operator, or repeat them using the * operator. Think of + like stapling two documents together, and * like a photocopier making multiple copies.

In forensics, concatenation is used to build file paths, construct search queries, assemble report sections, and combine data from multiple sources into a single output. The * operator is great for creating visual dividers in reports (like lines of dashes or equals signs).
key_concepts.example:
# Concatenation (+) and repetition (*)
first_name = "John"
last_name = "Doe"
full_name = first_name + " " + last_name
print("Suspect: " + full_name)

# Create a visual divider
divider = "=" * 40
print(divider)
print("FORENSIC ANALYSIS REPORT")
print(divider)
key_concepts.ai_hint: The + operator joins strings together. The * operator repeats a string. Together they're powerful for building formatted reports!

key_concepts.concept: print() Parameters — sep, end, and Multiple Arguments
key_concepts.explanation:
print() can take multiple arguments separated by commas, and has special parameters: sep (separator between items, default is a space) and end (what goes at the end, default is a newline). Think of sep like choosing whether to separate evidence items with commas, spaces, or dashes.

These parameters give you fine control over output formatting — essential for creating CSV-style output, custom log formats, and structured data exports that other tools can parse.
key_concepts.example:
# Multiple arguments with custom separator
print("IP", "192.168.1.1", "Port", "443", sep=" | ")

# Custom end parameter (no newline)
print("Scanning", end="...")
print("Done!")

# Print as CSV
print("timestamp", "source_ip", "action", sep=",")
print("2025-02-28", "10.0.0.1", "LOGIN_FAIL", sep=",")
key_concepts.ai_hint: sep controls what goes BETWEEN items, end controls what goes AFTER. Default sep is a space, default end is a new line.

key_concepts.explanation:
Unlike most programming languages, Python uses INDENTATION (spaces at the start of a line) to define code blocks. This isn't optional — it's the law! Use 4 spaces (or Tab) for each level. Think of indentation like the filing system in a forensic lab — everything must be in its correct place, or the whole system breaks down.

Coming from Java? In Java, curly braces {} define blocks. In Python, it's ALL about indentation. This makes Python code extremely readable — which is why forensic analysts love it for writing scripts that need to be reviewed and verified by others.
key_concepts.example:
# Indentation matters!
if True:
    print("This is indented correctly")
    print("This is also inside the if block")
print("This is OUTSIDE the if block")

# WRONG — this would cause an error:
# if True:
# print("Not indented = IndentationError!")
key_concepts.ai_hint: 4 spaces per level is the Python standard. Most code editors add them automatically when you press Tab. If you get an IndentationError, check your spacing!

key_concepts.concept: Python Syntax Rules — The Grammar of Code
key_concepts.explanation:
Python has rules just like any language: it's CASE SENSITIVE (Print ≠ print), statements end at the line break (no semicolons needed!), and variable names can't start with numbers or contain spaces. Think of these like the rules of evidence in court — break them and your case (code) gets thrown out.

Key rules: Python is case-sensitive (myVar ≠ myvar ≠ MYVAR). No semicolons needed at end of lines. Use meaningful variable names (evidence_file, not x). Python uses snake_case (words_separated_by_underscores) while Java uses camelCase (wordsSeparatedLikeThis).
key_concepts.example:
# Python is case-sensitive
name = "Alice"
Name = "Bob"
NAME = "Charlie"
print(name)   # Alice
print(Name)   # Bob
print(NAME)   # Charlie
# These are THREE different variables!

# No semicolons needed (but allowed)
print("No semicolon needed")
print("But this works too");
key_concepts.ai_hint: Python is case-sensitive! print() works, Print() doesn't. This trips up beginners all the time — you'll get used to it fast.

key_concepts.explanation:
Triple quotes (''' or """) create strings that span multiple lines. They're perfect for report templates, help text, ASCII art, and any output that needs to preserve line breaks and formatting exactly as you type it.

In forensics, multi-line strings are used for report templates, evidence summaries, banner messages, and structured output blocks. They're much cleaner than writing multiple print() statements.
key_concepts.example:
# Multi-line report template
report = """
╔══════════════════════════════════════╗
║     DIGITAL FORENSICS REPORT        ║
║     Case: CASE-2025-0042            ║
║     Status: IN PROGRESS             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════╝
"""
print(report)
key_concepts.ai_hint: Triple quotes are perfect for report templates! Write once, reuse everywhere. Professional forensic tools use these constantly.

key_concepts.explanation:
When Python can't understand your code, it gives you an error message. SyntaxError means you broke a grammar rule (missing quote, wrong bracket). NameError means you used a variable that doesn't exist. TypeError means you mixed incompatible types. Think of errors like clues — they tell you EXACTLY what went wrong and WHERE.

Reading error messages is a SKILL. The last line tells you the error type, and the ^ symbol points to where Python got confused. Forensic analysts debug code all the time — learning to read errors makes you faster and better.
key_concepts.example:
# Common errors (try these to see what happens!)
# print("Missing closing quote)
# print(Hello)  # NameError — needs quotes
# print("Age: " + 25)  # TypeError — can't add str + int

# The FIX:
print("Missing closing quote")
print("Hello")
print("Age: " + str(25))  # Convert int to str first
print(f"Age: {25}")  # Or use f-string (easier!)
key_concepts.ai_hint: Errors are NOT failures — they're Python giving you CLUES! Read the last line of the error message. It tells you exactly what happened.

key_concepts.concept: Python vs Java — Quick Comparison for Forensics
key_concepts.explanation:
If you've done the Java Security course, here's the key difference: Python prioritises SPEED of writing over formality. No class declarations, no main method, no semicolons, no type declarations. What takes 5 lines in Java takes 1 line in Python. This is why forensic analysts prefer Python for quick scripts — when you need to extract evidence NOW, you don't want to write boilerplate code.

However, Java is better for large applications (like Autopsy, Burp Suite). Python excels at quick scripts, automation, and glue code that connects tools together. Most security professionals know BOTH.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Python = quick scripts, fast prototyping, forensic tools. Java = large applications, enterprise security. Learn both to be unstoppable!

key_concepts.concept: Setting Up Your Forensics Lab (VM Recommendation)
key_concepts.explanation:
Throughout this Python course, we'll suggest real security tools you can try. For safety, ALWAYS use a Virtual Machine (VM). A VM is like a computer inside your computer — completely isolated. If something goes wrong, you just delete it.

Recommended setup: Download VirtualBox (free) from virtualbox.org, then download Kali Linux (free) from kali.org. Kali comes pre-loaded with 600+ security tools: Wireshark for network analysis, exiftool for image metadata, steghide for steganography, Nmap for scanning, John the Ripper for password analysis, and much more.

Synapse teaches you the CONCEPTS and PYTHON CODE safely. Your Kali VM is where you practice with real tools. NEVER run security tools on networks or systems you don't own — that's illegal in most countries.
key_concepts.example:
# You'll write Python code like this on Synapse:
print("=" * 40)
print("FORENSICS LAB SETUP CHECKLIST")
print("=" * 40)
print("1. Install VirtualBox (virtualbox.org)")
print("2. Download Kali Linux (kali.org)")
print("3. Create VM with 4GB RAM, 40GB disk")
print("4. Boot Kali and explore the tools!")
print("\nTools you'll use later:")
print("  - exiftool   (image metadata)")
print("  - steghide   (hidden messages)")
print("  - Wireshark  (network packets)")
print("  - Nmap       (port scanning)")
key_concepts.ai_hint: You don't NEED a VM to learn here — Synapse teaches you safely. But when you're ready for real tools, Kali Linux is the industry standard!

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Your Very First Python Program
complete_examples.description: The classic Hello World — but with a forensic twist
complete_examples.code:
# Your very first Python forensic script!
# Every expert started exactly here.

print("Welcome to Python Forensics!")
print("================================")
print("Today you become a digital investigator.")
print("Every line of code is a step forward.")
print("Let's begin the investigation!")
complete_examples.output:
Welcome to Python Forensics!
================================
Today you become a digital investigator.
Every line of code is a step forward.
Let's begin the investigation!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line 1-2: Comments (starting with #) — Python ignores these. They're notes for YOU.
Line 4: print() displays text on screen. The text must be inside quotes.
Line 5: We can use = characters to create a visual divider line.
Lines 6-8: More print() statements — each one appears on a new line automatically.

Every print() statement = one line of output. Python runs them top to bottom, in order.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try changing the messages to introduce yourself as a forensic investigator! Add your name and today's date.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Single vs Double vs Triple Quotes
complete_examples.description: Three ways to create strings — and when to use each one
complete_examples.code:
# Three types of quotes — all valid!

# Single quotes — quick and simple
print('Evidence collected successfully')

# Double quotes — useful when text contains apostrophes
print("The suspect's phone was recovered")

# Triple quotes — for multi-line text
print("""CASE SUMMARY:
Suspect: Unknown
Evidence: 3 devices
Status: Under investigation""")
complete_examples.output:
Evidence collected successfully
The suspect's phone was recovered
CASE SUMMARY:
Suspect: Unknown
Evidence: 3 devices
Status: Under investigation
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line 4: Single quotes work perfectly for simple text.
Line 7: Double quotes let you include apostrophes (') without breaking the string.
Lines 10-13: Triple quotes (three double quotes) preserve line breaks exactly as you type them.

Rule of thumb: Use single quotes by default. Switch to double when your text has apostrophes. Use triple for multi-line text.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write a print statement using single quotes that includes a double-quoted word inside it. Example: The file was marked "suspicious".

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Escape Characters — Special Codes
complete_examples.description: How to include newlines, tabs, quotes, and backslashes inside strings
complete_examples.code:
# Escape characters start with backslash \

# \n = new line
print("Line 1\nLine 2\nLine 3")

# \t = tab (indent)
print("\nEvidence Log:")
print("\tItem 1:\tLaptop")
print("\tItem 2:\tPhone")
print("\tItem 3:\tUSB Drive")

# \\ = actual backslash (needed for Windows paths)
print("\nFile path: C:\\Users\\suspect\\Documents\\secret.pdf")

# \" = quote inside double-quoted string
print("\nWitness said: \"I saw nothing\"")
complete_examples.output:
Line 1
Line 2
Line 3

Evidence Log:
	Item 1:	Laptop
	Item 2:	Phone
	Item 3:	USB Drive

File path: C:\Users\suspect\Documents\secret.pdf

Witness said: "I saw nothing"
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
\n = Forces a new line (like pressing Enter)
\t = Tab character (creates indentation, great for structured output)
\\ = Prints a single backslash (needed because \ is the escape character itself)
\" = Prints a quote mark inside a double-quoted string

Think of \ as saying 'the NEXT character is special'. Without the backslash, Python would think the quote ends the string.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create a mini evidence report using \n for new lines and \t for indentation. Include a file path with backslashes!

complete_examples.title: Example 4: f-Strings — Dynamic Forensic Output
complete_examples.description: The modern way to embed variables in text — essential for forensic reports
complete_examples.code:
# f-strings: put f before the quote, variables in {}

case_id = "CASE-2025-0042"
investigator = "Agent Smith"
evidence_count = 7
urgency = "HIGH"

print(f"Case ID: {case_id}")
print(f"Lead Investigator: {investigator}")
print(f"Evidence Items: {evidence_count}")
print(f"Priority: {urgency}")
print(f"Remaining items to process: {evidence_count - 2}")

# You can even do calculations inside {}
print(f"\nProgress: {5}/{evidence_count} = {5/evidence_count*100:.1f}%")
complete_examples.output:
Case ID: CASE-2025-0042
Lead Investigator: Agent Smith
Evidence Items: 7
Priority: HIGH
Remaining items to process: 5

Progress: 5/7 = 71.4%
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line 3-6: We create variables to hold our data (text and numbers).
Line 8: f"..." is an f-string. The f tells Python to look for {} and replace them with variable values.
Line 12: You can do MATH inside {} — here we subtract 2 from evidence_count.
Line 15: :.1f formats the number to 1 decimal place. This is how forensic tools display percentages!

f-strings are the #1 way to format output in modern Python. Master these!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create your own case report using f-strings with at least 4 different variables. Include a calculation inside one of the {} blocks!

complete_examples.title: Example 5: String Concatenation & Repetition
complete_examples.description: Joining and repeating strings for formatted output
complete_examples.code:
# String concatenation (+) joins strings together
first = "Digital"
second = "Forensics"
full = first + " " + second
print(full)

# String repetition (*) repeats a string
line = "═" * 40
print(line)
print("   EVIDENCE ANALYSIS REPORT")
print(line)

# Combine both!
warning = "⚠ " * 3 + "CLASSIFIED" + " ⚠" * 3
print(warning)

# Build a formatted table header
print("\n" + "-" * 50)
print("| {:>5} | {:>15} | {:>15} |".format("ID", "Filename", "Hash"))
print("-" * 50)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line 2-4: The + operator JOINS strings. You need to manually add spaces.
Line 7: The * operator REPEATS a string — '═' * 40 creates 40 equal signs.
Line 13: You can combine + and * in one expression!
Line 16-18: .format() is an older way to format strings (still works, but f-strings are usually better).

Professional forensic tools use these techniques to create clean, readable reports.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create a box around text using + and * operators. Make it look like: ╔══════╗ on top, ║ TEXT ║ in the middle, ╚══════╝ on the bottom.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: print() Parameters — sep and end
complete_examples.description: Fine-tuning your output with separator and end parameters
complete_examples.code:
# sep parameter: what goes BETWEEN items
print("2025", "02", "28", sep="-")
print("192", "168", "1", "1", sep=".")

# end parameter: what goes at the END (default is \n)
print("Scanning", end="... ")
print("Done!")

# Combine sep and end
print("Evidence", "collected", sep="_", end=".txt\n")

# Create CSV-style output
print("\nTimestamp,Source IP,Action,Result")
for i in range(1, 4):
    print(f"2025-02-28 10:{i:02d}", f"192.168.1.{i}", "LOGIN", "FAIL", sep=",")
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line 2: sep='-' puts a dash between each item → great for dates!
Line 3: sep='.' puts dots between items → perfect for IP addresses.
Line 6: end='... ' replaces the default newline, so the NEXT print continues on the SAME line.
Line 10: Combine both! sep='_' joins with underscore, end='.txt\n' adds the file extension.
Lines 13-15: A loop creating CSV output — each line uses comma as separator. This is how log parsers work!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create a colon-separated output for MAC addresses (like AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF) using print() with sep=':'

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Multi-line Report with Triple Quotes
complete_examples.description: Building a professional forensic report template
complete_examples.code:
# Professional report template using triple quotes + f-strings

case_num = "FR-2025-0189"
analyst = "Your Name"
date = "2025-02-28"
device = "iPhone 15 Pro"
imei = "352789102345678"
files_found = 1247
suspicious = 23

report = f"""
╔{'═' * 48}╗
║{'DIGITAL FORENSICS — EVIDENCE REPORT':^48}║
╠{'═' * 48}╣
║  Case Number:   {case_num:<30}║
║  Analyst:       {analyst:<30}║
║  Date:          {date:<30}║
╠{'═' * 48}╣
║  Device:        {device:<30}║
║  IMEI:          {imei:<30}║
║  Files Found:   {files_found:<30}║
║  Suspicious:    {suspicious:<30}║
╠{'═' * 48}╣
║  STATUS: {'ANALYSIS IN PROGRESS':^38}║
╚{'═' * 48}╝
"""

print(report)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This combines EVERYTHING you've learned!
- Triple quotes (""" """) for multi-line text
- f-string (f before the quotes) to embed variables
- {variable:<30} left-aligns text in a 30-character wide field
- {:^48} centres text in a 48-character field
- {'═' * 48} repeats the box-drawing character 48 times

This is exactly how professional forensic tools format their output. You just built your first real report template!

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Colour Coding with ANSI Escape Codes
complete_examples.description: Add colours to terminal output — essential for forensic tool UX
complete_examples.code:
# ANSI escape codes add colour to terminal output!
# Format: \033[CODEm  where CODE is a colour number

# Define colours
RED = "\033[91m"
GREEN = "\033[92m"
YELLOW = "\033[93m"
BLUE = "\033[94m"
PURPLE = "\033[95m"
CYAN = "\033[96m"
RESET = "\033[0m"  # Reset to default
BOLD = "\033[1m"

# Use them in print statements
print(f"{BOLD}{CYAN}=== VULNERABILITY SCAN RESULTS ==={RESET}")
print(f"{RED}[CRITICAL]{RESET} SQL Injection found in /login")
print(f"{RED}[CRITICAL]{RESET} XSS found in /search")
print(f"{YELLOW}[WARNING]{RESET}  Outdated SSL certificate")
print(f"{GREEN}[OK]{RESET}       Firewall rules verified")
print(f"{GREEN}[OK]{RESET}       HTTPS enforced")
print(f"\n{BOLD}Summary: {RED}2 Critical{RESET}, {YELLOW}1 Warning{RESET}, {GREEN}2 OK{RESET}")
complete_examples.output:
=== VULNERABILITY SCAN RESULTS ===
[CRITICAL] SQL Injection found in /login
[CRITICAL] XSS found in /search
[WARNING]  Outdated SSL certificate
[OK]       Firewall rules verified
[OK]       HTTPS enforced

Summary: 2 Critical, 1 Warning, 2 OK
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
ANSI escape codes are special sequences that terminals understand as colour instructions.
\033[ starts the sequence, the number selects the colour, and m ends it.
91=red, 92=green, 93=yellow, 94=blue, 95=purple, 96=cyan
\033[0m resets back to normal (ALWAYS reset after colouring!)
\033[1m makes text bold.

Most professional forensic tools use colour coding: red for critical findings, yellow for warnings, green for OK. On Kali Linux, tools like Nmap and Nikto use these exact techniques!

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Hex and ASCII — The Language of Digital Evidence
complete_examples.description: Preview of what's coming — converting between text and hex/ASCII codes
complete_examples.code:
# Preview: Hex and ASCII — you'll master these later!
# Every digital file is made of bytes (numbers 0-255)
# ASCII maps numbers to characters

# Convert text to ASCII codes
message = "HACK"
print("Text to ASCII codes:")
for char in message:
    ascii_code = ord(char)  # ord() gets the ASCII number
    hex_code = hex(ascii_code)  # hex() converts to hexadecimal
    binary = bin(ascii_code)  # bin() converts to binary
    print(f"  '{char}' = ASCII {ascii_code:>3} = Hex {hex_code} = Binary {binary}")

# Convert ASCII codes back to text
print("\nASCII codes back to text:")
codes = [72, 69, 76, 76, 79]
result = ""
for code in codes:
    result += chr(code)  # chr() converts number to character
print(f"  {codes} = '{result}'")

# Hex string to text
hex_message = "48 65 6C 6C 6F"
print(f"\nHex '{hex_message}' = ", end="")
for h in hex_message.split():
    print(chr(int(h, 16)), end="")
print()
complete_examples.output:
Text to ASCII codes:
  'H' = ASCII  72 = Hex 0x48 = Binary 0b1001000
  'A' = ASCII  65 = Hex 0x41 = Binary 0b1000001
  'C' = ASCII  67 = Hex 0x43 = Binary 0b1000011
  'K' = ASCII  75 = Hex 0x4b = Binary 0b1001011

ASCII codes back to text:
  [72, 69, 76, 76, 79] = 'HELLO'

Hex '48 65 6C 6C 6F' = Hello
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
ord(char) — Converts a character to its ASCII number (A=65, B=66, etc.)
chr(number) — Converts an ASCII number back to a character
hex(number) — Shows the hexadecimal representation (base 16)
bin(number) — Shows the binary representation (base 2)
int(hex_string, 16) — Converts a hex string to a decimal number

Why does this matter? EVERY file on a computer is stored as hex bytes. Forensic analysts read hex dumps daily! On Kali, try: xxd filename | head to see any file as hex.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Complete Forensic Report Generator
complete_examples.description: Putting it ALL together — a full report that uses every technique from this topic
complete_examples.code:
# === COMPLETE FORENSIC REPORT GENERATOR ===
# Uses: print, f-strings, escape chars, multi-line,
# concatenation, sep, end, comments, variables

import datetime

# Configuration
CASE = "CASE-FR-2025-0042"
ANALYST = "Synapse Student"
DATE = datetime.datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")
CLASSIFICATION = "CONFIDENTIAL"

# Colour codes (work in terminals)
R = "\033[91m"; G = "\033[92m"; Y = "\033[93m"
B = "\033[94m"; C = "\033[96m"; X = "\033[0m"; BOLD = "\033[1m"

# Header
print(f"{BOLD}{C}{'=' * 56}{X}")
print(f"{BOLD}{C}{'SYNAPSE DIGITAL FORENSICS REPORT':^56}{X}")
print(f"{BOLD}{C}{'=' * 56}{X}")

# Case info
print(f"\n{B}Case:{X}       {CASE}")
print(f"{B}Analyst:{X}    {ANALYST}")
print(f"{B}Date:{X}       {DATE}")
print(f"{B}Class:{X}      {R}{CLASSIFICATION}{X}")

# Findings
findings = [
    ("CRITICAL", "SQL Injection", "/api/login", "CWE-89"),
    ("CRITICAL", "XSS Stored", "/comments", "CWE-79"),
    ("HIGH", "Weak Password Hash", "/auth/users", "CWE-328"),
    ("MEDIUM", "Info Disclosure", "/debug/errors", "CWE-209"),
    ("LOW", "Missing Header", "All pages", "CWE-693"),
]

print(f"\n{BOLD}{'─' * 56}{X}")
print(f"{BOLD}{'SEVERITY':<12}{'VULNERABILITY':<20}{'LOCATION':<16}{'CWE':<10}{X}")
print(f"{'─' * 56}")

for sev, vuln, loc, cwe in findings:
    if sev == "CRITICAL":
        colour = R
    elif sev == "HIGH":
        colour = Y
    elif sev == "MEDIUM":
        colour = Y
    else:
        colour = G
    print(f"{colour}{sev:<12}{X}{vuln:<20}{loc:<16}{cwe:<10}")

print(f"{'─' * 56}")
print(f"\nTotal: {R}2 Critical{X}, {Y}1 High{X}, {Y}1 Medium{X}, {G}1 Low{X}")
print(f"\n{B}Next Steps:{X}")
print(f"  1. Patch critical vulnerabilities immediately")
print(f"  2. Schedule remediation for HIGH/MEDIUM")
print(f"  3. Include LOW fixes in next release cycle")
print(f"\n{C}Report generated by Synapse Forensics Toolkit{X}")
print(f"{C}{'=' * 56}{X}")

visual_diagram.title: Python Execution Flow & Output Reference
visual_diagram.content:
🐍 HOW PYTHON EXECUTES YOUR CODE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FROM CODE TO OUTPUT — STEP BY STEP
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
📝 You Write Code
│
▼
🔍 Python Reads Line 1 ← Top to bottom, one line at a time
│
▼
❓ Is it a comment (#)?
YES → Skip it │ NO → Process it
│
▼
❓ Syntax correct?
NO → SyntaxError! 🚨 │ YES → Continue ✓
│
▼
⚡ Execute the Line
│
▼
❓ Is it print()?
YES → Display on screen 📺 │ NO → Do other action
│
▼
❓ More lines?
YES → Go to next line ↑ │ NO → Program ends ✅
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
print() PARAMETER REFERENCE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
print(value1, value2, sep=" ", end="\n")
sep = Separator between values (default: space) print("A", "B", sep="-") → A-B print("192", "168", "1", "1", sep=".") → 192.168.1.1 end = What goes at the end (default: newline) print("Loading", end="...") → Loading... (no line break)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ESCAPE CHARACTER CHEAT SHEET
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
\n New line (like pressing Enter) \t Tab (indent) \\ Backslash (literal \) \" Double quote inside double-quoted string \' Single quote inside single-quoted string
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
💡 REMEMBER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✓ Python runs code TOP to BOTTOM ✓ Comments (#) are ignored ✓ Strings need matching quotes (' ' or " ") ✓ f-strings: f"text {variable}" for dynamic output ✓ Python is CASE SENSITIVE (print ≠ Print) ✓ Indentation = 4 spaces (Python's golden rule) ✓ Use Kali Linux VM for real security tools!
